result.markdown:
--- title: "Module 6: Useful R packages" subtitle: "gt · gtsummary · sjPlot · table1 · likert · janitor · rio · here · pacman" author: "Alex Edwards & Zane Billings" date: today format: revealjs: theme: simple slide-number: true scrollable: true transition: fade code-line-numbers: true highlight-style: github incremental: false toc: false execute: eval: false echo: true editor: markdown: wrap: 72 --- # Welcome ## Agenda {.smaller} A one-hour tour of the R packages that take you from **messy data** to **publication-ready tables and reports** — demonstrated on a real, de-identified ICU dataset. ## Agenda {.smaller} | Time | Topic | |-------|-------------------------------------------| | 2 min | Why these packages? | | 2 min | The dataset: QCRC ICU cohort | | 3 min | `pacman::p_load()` — loading packages | | 3 min | `here` — project-relative file paths | | 6 min | `rio` — import/export anything | | 6 min | `janitor::clean_names()` — tidy names | | 7 min | `table1` — "Table 1" | | 6 min | `gt` — grammar of tables | | 8 min | `gtsummary` — summary & regression tables | | 8 min | `sjPlot` — `tab_model()` & `tab_xtab()` | | 6 min | `likert` — ordinal clinical scores | | 3 min | Wrap-up & resources | ## Why these packages? - Every analysis ends with a table, a report, or a slide someone else has to read - Base R output (`summary()`, `str()`) is fast to write but ugly to share - These packages sit **on top of** your normal analysis and turn it into something you can paste into a paper, a report, or an email ## Which packages? ::: incremental - `here`, `rio`, `janitor` → find, load, and clean your data - `table1` → describe your sample - `gt`, `gtsummary`, `sjPlot` → build the tables people actually read - `likert` → visualize ordered/scale data - `pacman` → glue that loads everything with one line ::: ------------------------------------------------------------------------ ## About the Dataset Every example in this deck uses the same real, de-identified file: **`QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx`** - 288 patients admitted to the ICU with COVID-19 - 10 sheets — `Main_Dataset` (demographics, treatments, outcomes), daily `SOFA` organ-failure scores, `Long_Labs`, `Long_Vitals`, ventilator settings, medications, and more - We'll mainly use `Main_Dataset`: 37 variables including age, sex, race, BMI, intubation, CRRT/dialysis, and mortality - 98 of 288 patients (34%) died — a natural grouping variable for every table in this lecture ::: notes Before running this deck live: save the workbook as `data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx` inside your project folder — the `here()` and `rio::import()` calls later assume that path. ::: # Setup: `pacman::p_load()` ## The problem with `library()` A typical analysis script starts like this: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(here) library(rio) library(janitor) library(table1) library(gt) library(gtsummary) library(sjPlot) library(likert) library(tidyverse) ``` ::: If **one** of these isn't installed, the whole script stops with an error — and you have to go install it manually, then re-run. ## `pacman::p_load()` `pacman` is a package-management package. Its star function, **`p_load()`**, installs anything missing, then loads everything — in one line. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # install.packages("pacman") # only needed once pacman::p_load( here, rio, janitor, table1, gt, gtsummary, sjPlot, likert, tidyverse ) ``` ::: ## `pacman::p_load()` - Checks each package name - Installs it from CRAN **only if missing** - Loads (`library()`s) all of them - Great for shared scripts, teaching, and reproducibility setups # `here`: Project-Relative Paths ## The problem with `setwd()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # fragile — breaks the moment this script runs on # a different machine, or is Source()'d vs rendered setwd("~/Downloads/SISMID2026") qcrc <- import("data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx") ``` ::: ## `here::here()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(here) #This is where it think we are here() #This gets us to our data here("data", "QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx") #How do we read in data qcrcHere <- import(here("data", "QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx")) ``` ::: ## `here::here()` - Finds your project root automatically (looks for a `.Rproj` file, `.git` folder, or similar) and builds paths **from there** - The same `here()` call works identically whether you run it from RStudio, the terminal, or a rendered Quarto document - Pairs perfectly with `rio::import()` — see next section ::: notes Rule of thumb: replace every `setwd()` and every hard-coded absolute path with `here()`. ::: # `rio`: Import & Export Anything ## One function in, one function out `rio` wraps dozens of format-specific packages (`haven`, `readxl`, `data.table`, `jsonlite`, ...) behind two simple verbs. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(rio) library(here) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` here() starts at /Users/xpf100/Downloads/SISMID2026 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} qcrc <- import( here("data", "QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx"), sheet = "Main_Dataset" ) dim(qcrc) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 288 37 ``` ::: ::: ## One function in, one function out No more remembering `read_csv()` vs `read.spss()` vs `read_excel()` — and no hard-coded paths, thanks to `here()`. ## Exporting is just as easy ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} export(qcrc, here("data", "qcrc_clean.xlsx")) export(qcrc, here("data", "qcrc_clean.rds")) export(qcrc, here("data", "qcrc_clean.csv")) ``` ::: ## Exporting is just as easy - Format is inferred from the file extension - Supports 30+ formats: `.csv`, `.xlsx`, `.sav`, `.dta`, `.sas7bdat`, `.json`, `.rds`, `.parquet`, and more - `import_list()` reads **every sheet** at once — perfect for this workbook, which has 10 sheets: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sheets <- import_list(here("data", "QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx")) names(sheets) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "Main_Dataset" "Oxygen_Delivery" "SOFA" [4] "Long_Labs" "Long_Vitals" "Static_Compliance" [7] "PAO2_FIO2" "ICU_Meds" "Intake_Form" [10] "Daily_Form" ``` ::: ::: ::: notes Good moment for a live demo — scroll through `sheets` to show the whole workbook loaded in one call. ::: # `janitor`: Clean Names Fast ## Messy names are the #1 headache ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} names(qcrc) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "Patient_DEID" "Decatur_Transfer" [3] "Age" "Female" [5] "Race" "Died" [7] "30D_Mortality" "60D_Mortality" [9] "Death date_deid" "ICU_LOS" [11] "BMI" "Died in ICU" [13] "Code status" "Epoprostenol" [15] "Remdesivir_or_placebo" "Intubated" [17] "ICU_ADMIT_DEID" "ICU_DC_DEID" [19] "HOSPITAL_ADMIT_DEID" "HOSPITAL_DC_DEID" [21] "Vent LOS" "CRRT" [23] "CRRT days" "HD" [25] "HD days" "Pressor >2 hours" [27] "Pressor days" "Absolute lymphocyte" [29] "WBC" "D dimer" [31] "CRP" "Peak troponin" [33] "P:F ratio at ICU admission" "P:F ratio at first intubation" [35] "Calculated_Cstat" "First_Intubation_deid" [37] "Last_Intubation_deid" ``` ::: ::: ## Messy names are the #1 headache Notice the inconsistency: `Title Case With Spaces` (`Vent LOS`, `Peak troponin`), `ALL_CAPS_WITH_UNDERSCORES` (`ICU_ADMIT_DEID`), and even a colon (`P:F ratio at ICU admission`). All are painful to type or reference in code. ## `clean_names()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(janitor) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'janitor' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': chisq.test, fisher.test ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} qcrc_clean <- qcrc |> clean_names() names(qcrc_clean) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "patient_deid" "decatur_transfer" [3] "age" "female" [5] "race" "died" [7] "x30d_mortality" "x60d_mortality" [9] "death_date_deid" "icu_los" [11] "bmi" "died_in_icu" [13] "code_status" "epoprostenol" [15] "remdesivir_or_placebo" "intubated" [17] "icu_admit_deid" "icu_dc_deid" [19] "hospital_admit_deid" "hospital_dc_deid" [21] "vent_los" "crrt" [23] "crrt_days" "hd" [25] "hd_days" "pressor_2_hours" [27] "pressor_days" "absolute_lymphocyte" [29] "wbc" "d_dimer" [31] "crp" "peak_troponin" [33] "p_f_ratio_at_icu_admission" "p_f_ratio_at_first_intubation" [35] "calculated_cstat" "first_intubation_deid" [37] "last_intubation_deid" ``` ::: ::: ## `clean_names()` - Converts everything to consistent `snake_case` - Strips spaces and punctuation (even the colon), collapses repeated separators, and prefixes names that start with a digit (`30D_...` → `x30d_...`) - One line, run right after `import()` — every variable name used for the rest of this lecture assumes this has already happened ## Bonus: fixing the `"."` missing code {.smaller} Like a lot of clinical exports, this dataset stores missing values as a literal `"."` string — which leaves numeric columns like `bmi`, `wbc`, and `crp` stuck as text. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(dplyr) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'dplyr' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': filter, lag ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following objects are masked from 'package:base': intersect, setdiff, setequal, union ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} num_cols <- c("bmi", "wbc", "d_dimer", "crp", "peak_troponin", "calculated_cstat") qcrc_clean <- qcrc_clean |> mutate(across(all_of(num_cols), ~ na_if(as.character(.x), ".")|> as.numeric())) |> mutate(died = factor(died, labels = c("Survived", "Died"))) ``` ::: ## Bonus: fixing the `"."` missing code {.smaller} Not a janitor function, but the natural next step after `clean_names()` — tidy the *names*, then tidy the *values*. This is the `qcrc_clean` object every later slide builds on. # `table1`: Describing Your Sample ## The "Table 1" problem Nearly every clinical paper starts with a baseline characteristics table split by outcome or treatment group — this is almost always called **"Table 1."** Our cohort: 288 ICU patients, 98 of whom (34%) died. Building it by hand means computing means, SDs, counts, percentages, and p-values separately for every variable. ## `table1()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(table1) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'table1' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following objects are masked from 'package:base': units, units<- ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} #Table One No Strata table1(~age+bmi+icu_los+intubated+crrt, data=qcrc_clean) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Overall
(N=288)
age
Mean (SD) 63.5 (15.5)
Median [Min, Max] 65.0 [19.0, 89.0]
bmi
Mean (SD) 31.6 (8.34)
Median [Min, Max] 30.3 [15.4, 64.8]
Missing 3 (1.0%)
icu_los
Mean (SD) 10.5 (8.76)
Median [Min, Max] 9.00 [0, 56.0]
intubated
Mean (SD) 0.736 (0.442)
Median [Min, Max] 1.00 [0, 1.00]
crrt
Mean (SD) 0.229 (0.421)
Median [Min, Max] 0 [0, 1.00]
``` ::: ::: ## `table1()` - Older age, lower BMI, mechanical ventilation, and CRRT (dialysis) were all significantly associated with death in this cohort - Printed table can be coerced to a data frame and then piped into `gt` for a polished, exportable version. ## `table1() with strata` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} #Table one with strata table1(~age+bmi+icu_los+intubated+crrt|female, data=qcrc_clean) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Female

``` ::: ::: ## `table1() with two strata` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} #Table One with two strata table1(~age+bmi+icu_los+intubated+crrt|female*died, data=qcrc_clean) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Female
Male
Overall
Survived
(N=88)		 Died
(N=43)		 Survived
(N=102)		 Died
(N=55)		 Survived
(N=190)		 Died
(N=98)

Mean (SD) 60.9 (17.2) 69.5 (16.1) 59.2 (13.2) 70.8 (12.0) 60.0 (15.2) 70.2 (13.9)
Median [Min, Max] 60.5 [21.0, 89.0] 70.0 [23.0, 89.0] 61.0 [19.0, 89.0] 71.0 [32.0, 89.0] 61.0 [19.0, 89.0] 71.0 [23.0, 89.0]
bmi
Mean (SD) 34.2 (9.57) 29.1 (6.96) 32.4 (8.07) 28.0 (5.75) 33.2 (8.82) 28.5 (6.30)
Median [Min, Max] 31.6 [20.4, 64.8] 29.3 [16.4, 50.3] 31.2 [17.6, 64.7] 27.0 [15.4, 40.0] 31.4 [17.6, 64.8] 28.5 [15.4, 50.3]
Missing 2 (2.3%) 0 (0%) 1 (1.0%) 0 (0%) 3 (1.6%) 0 (0%)
icu_los
Mean (SD) 10.1 (10.3) 9.45 (7.53) 10.8 (7.73) 11.5 (8.80) 10.5 (9.02) 10.6 (8.29)
Median [Min, Max] 7.00 [0, 56.0] 8.00 [0, 28.0] 9.16 [0.435, 39.0] 9.00 [0, 41.0] 8.36 [0, 56.0] 9.00 [0, 41.0]

Mean (SD) 0.682 (0.468) 0.744 (0.441) 0.696 (0.462) 0.891 (0.315) 0.689 (0.464) 0.827 (0.381)
Median [Min, Max] 1.00 [0, 1.00] 1.00 [0, 1.00] 1.00 [0, 1.00] 1.00 [0, 1.00] 1.00 [0, 1.00] 1.00 [0, 1.00]

Mean (SD) 0.0909 (0.289) 0.256 (0.441) 0.167 (0.375) 0.545 (0.503) 0.132 (0.339) 0.418 (0.496)

``` ::: ::: # `gt`: A Grammar of Tables ## Any data frame → a polished table ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(gt) qcrc_clean |> select(patient_deid, age, female, race, bmi, icu_los, died) |> head(6) |> gt() |> tab_header( title = "QCRC ICU Cohort", subtitle = "First 6 patients" ) |> fmt_number(columns = c(bmi, icu_los), decimals = 1) |> cols_label(patient_deid = "Patient ID", icu_los = "ICU LOS (days)") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
QCRC ICU Cohort
First 6 patients
Patient ID age female race bmi ICU LOS (days) died
17205428 89 Female Caucasian or White 26.2 11.1 Died
17234405 73 Female African American or Black 40.7 25.0 Survived
17239956 53 Male Caucasian or White 38.6 17.0 Survived
17350926 55 Male African American or Black 35.2 21.0 Died
17377951 66 Female African American or Black 32.9 11.2 Died
17397040 78 Male African American or Black 28.7 6.0 Died
``` ::: ::: ## Any data frame → a polished table - `gt()` turns a data frame into a table object - Everything after is a **layer**: header, formatting, labels, footnotes, spanners, styling — piped together ## Why `gt` matters - It's the **rendering engine** many other packages (including `gtsummary`) build on top of - Output looks the same in HTML, PDF, Word, and RTF - Useful layers to know: - `tab_spanner()` — group columns under a shared header - `tab_source_note()` — add a footnote/citation - `gtsave(tbl, "table.png")` — export to image, HTML, RTF, or Word # `gtsummary`: Summary & Regression Tables ## `tbl_summary()` — descriptive tables ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(gtsummary) qcrc_clean |> select(age, bmi, wbc, crp, died) |> tbl_summary( by = died, statistic = list(all_continuous() ~ "{median} ({p25}, {p75})") ) |> add_p() |> add_overall() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Characteristic Overall
N = 2881		 Survived
N = 1901		 Died
N = 981		 p-value2
age 65 (55, 75) 61 (51, 69) 71 (64, 79) <0.001
bmi 30 (26, 35) 31 (27, 37) 29 (25, 31) <0.001
    Unknown 3 3 0
wbc 8.6 (6.2, 11.7) 8.5 (6.2, 10.7) 9.5 (6.2, 12.7) 0.2
    Unknown 5 2 3
crp 169 (111, 234) 171 (109, 229) 169 (113, 246) 0.6
    Unknown 31 14 17
1 Median (Q1, Q3)
2 Wilcoxon rank sum test
``` ::: ::: ## `tbl_summary()` — descriptive tables - Median (IQR) instead of mean (SD) — a better default for skewed labs like CRP and WBC - Same goal as `table1`, but the output is already a polished **gt table** — ready to drop into a report - `add_p()` adds group-comparison p-values; `add_overall()` adds a combined column ## `tbl_regression()` — model tables ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mod <- glm(died ~ age + bmi + intubated + crrt, data = qcrc_clean, family = binomial) tbl_regression(mod, exponentiate = TRUE) |> add_glance_source_note() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Characteristic OR 95% CI p-value
age 1.05 1.03, 1.07 <0.001
bmi 0.92 0.87, 0.96 <0.001
intubated 1.93 0.94, 4.10 0.078
crrt 6.57 3.28, 13.7 <0.001
Abbreviations: CI = Confidence Interval, OR = Odds Ratio
Null deviance = 367; Null df = 284; Log-likelihood = -142; AIC = 295; BIC = 313; Deviance = 285; Residual df = 280; No. Obs. = 285
``` ::: ::: ## `tbl_regression()` — model tables - Turns any `lm`/`glm`/`coxph`/mixed model into a clean estimate table (odds ratio, CI, p-value) — `exponentiate = TRUE` reports odds ratios instead of log-odds - `add_glance_source_note()` appends model fit stats (AIC, n) - `tbl_merge()` and `tbl_stack()` combine multiple models/tables side by side or on top of each other — handy for multi-model comparisons ::: notes gtsummary is the "batteries included" option — sensible defaults, statistical tests built in, minimal code. This is the same `died ~ age + bmi + intubated + crrt` model sjPlot will show next, just formatted differently. ::: # `sjPlot`: Regression & Crosstab Tables ## `tab_model()` — regression output ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(sjPlot) tab_model( mod, show.ci = TRUE, transform = "exp", dv.labels = "Died (ICU, 0/1)" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
  Died (ICU, 0/1)
Predictors Odds Ratios CI p
(Intercept) 0.11 0.00 – Inf 0.070
age 1.05 0.00 – Inf <0.001
bmi 0.92 0.00 – Inf <0.001
intubated 1.93 0.00 – Inf 0.078
crrt 6.57 0.00 – Inf <0.001
Observations 285
R2 Tjur 0.263
````` ::: ::: ## `tab_model()` — regression output - Formats coefficients, CIs, and p-values automatically — here as odds ratios (`transform = "exp"`) since this is a logistic model - Works with `lm`, `glm`, `lmer`, `coxph`, and more - Pass **multiple models** as extra arguments to compare them side by side in one table ## `tab_xtab()` — cross-tabulations ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} tab_xtab( var.row = qcrc_clean$race, var.col = qcrc_clean$died, show.row.prc = TRUE, show.summary = TRUE ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
race died Total
Survived Died
African American or
Black		 130
65.7 %		 68
34.3 %		 198
100 %
Asian 4
44.4 %		 5
55.6 %		 9
100 %
Caucasian or White 38
70.4 %		 16
29.6 %		 54
100 %
Multiple 0
0 %		 1
100 %		 1
100 %
Unknown, Unavailable
or Unreported		 18
69.2 %		 8
30.8 %		 26
100 %
Total 190
66 %		 98
34 %		 288
100 %
χ2=4.394 · df=4 · Cramer's V=0.124 · Fisher's p=0.354
````` ::: ::: - Builds a two-way frequency table for two categorical variables - `show.row.prc` / `show.col.prc` add row/column percentages - `show.summary` appends the chi-square test statistic and p-value - Same polished HTML output style as `tab_model()` ::: notes If time is short, this is the pair most useful to clinical audiences: one function for regression, one for crosstabs, both publication-ready with zero manual formatting. ::: # `likert`: Ordinal / Scale Data ## Beyond survey questions The `likert` package is built for survey agreement scales, but it works for **any** ordered categorical variable measured on a shared scale. The `SOFA` sheet has six organ-system subscores, each rated 0 (normal) to 4 (severe failure) — structurally identical to a 5-point Likert item. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sofa <- import( here("data", "QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx"), sheet = "SOFA" ) |> clean_names() lvls <- as.character(0:4) sofa_items <- sofa |> transmute( Coagulation = factor(coag, levels = lvls), Liver = factor(liver, levels = lvls), CNS = factor(cns, levels = lvls), Renal = factor(renal, levels = lvls), Cardiovascular = factor(cvs, levels = lvls), Respiratory = factor(resp, levels = lvls) ) ``` ::: ## `likert()` + `plot()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(likert) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Loading required package: ggplot2 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'ggplot2' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following object is masked from 'package:sjPlot': set_theme ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Loading required package: xtable ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'xtable' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following objects are masked from 'package:table1': label, label<- ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Registered S3 method overwritten by 'plyr': method from [.indexed table1 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'likert' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr': recode ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} l <- likert(as.data.frame(sofa_items)) summary(l) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Item low neutral high mean sd 5 Cardiovascular 39.58333 7.986111 52.430556 2.753472 1.4185144 4 Renal 42.70833 10.416667 46.875000 3.187500 1.7961195 6 Respiratory 50.00000 25.694444 24.305556 2.364583 1.3391957 3 CNS 83.33333 6.944444 9.722222 1.506944 1.0492840 1 Coagulation 96.18056 2.430556 1.388889 1.208333 0.5828561 2 Liver 98.61111 1.388889 0.000000 1.097222 0.3405150 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plot(l) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Warning: The `size` argument of `element_rect()` is deprecated as of ggplot2 3.4.0. ℹ Please use the `linewidth` argument instead. ℹ The deprecated feature was likely used in the likert package. Please report the issue at . ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](Module06-UsefulPackages_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-21-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: - Renal and cardiovascular subscores show the most severe dysfunction in this cohort (large "3"/"4" categories) - `summary()` gives the % of patients per score, per organ system; `plot()` produces the classic diverging stacked bar chart - The exact same workflow applies to real Likert survey items — agreement scales are just another ordered factor with a shared level set # Putting It Together ## Which package for which job? {.smaller} | Package | Best for | |-------------|------------------------------------------------------------| | `pacman` | Loading/installing packages in one line | | `here` | Project-relative file paths, no more `setwd()` | | `rio` | Reading/writing any data file format | | `janitor` | Cleaning column names & quick data checks | | `table1` | Descriptive "Table 1" split by group | | `gt` | Full control over any custom table's look | | `gtsummary` | Fast, statistically-aware summary & model tables | | `sjPlot` | Regression tables (`tab_model`) and crosstabs (`tab_xtab`) | | `likert` | Visualizing ordered survey/scale data | ## A typical workflow ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} pacman::p_load(here, rio, janitor, table1, gt, gtsummary, sjPlot, likert) qcrc <- import(here("data", "QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx"), sheet = "Main_Dataset") |> clean_names() table1(~age+bmi+female+icu_los+intubated+crrt|died, data=qcrc_clean) qcrc |> tbl_summary(by = died) mod <- glm(died ~ age + intubated + crrt, data = qcrc, family = binomial) tab_model(mod) ``` ::: Six packages, six lines, from a raw ICU spreadsheet to publication-ready output. # Wrap-Up ## Resources - `here`: - `rio`: - `janitor`: - `table1`: CRAN vignette — `vignette("introduction", package = "table1")` - `gt`: - `gtsummary`: - `sjPlot`: - `likert`: CRAN vignette — `vignette("likert", package = "likert")` - `pacman`: ## Questions?

<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
